metadata.title: Test Automation Bias by Running the Same Output With and Without Review with Claude

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The Stanford SCALE finding — that reading the agent's brief first makes me accept a wrong summary forty percent more often — is one I want to run on myself, not just believe. Help me: (1) produce a correct summary and one with a reversed key finding that still reads fluently; (2) build a five-claim checklist that forces me to verify each claim against the source paragraphs; (3) test condition A, agent-output-first, against condition B, source-first, and show me which claim the checklist catches.

props.command: The Stanford SCALE finding — that reading the agent's brief first makes me accept a wrong summary forty percent more often — is one I want to run on myself, not just believe. Help me: (1) produce a correct summary and one with a reversed key finding that still reads fluently; (2) build a five-claim checklist that forces me to verify each claim against the source paragraphs; (3) test condition A, agent-output-first, against condition B, source-first, and show me which claim the checklist catches.
props.segment: Test Automation Bias by Running the

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

lines: Test Automation Bias Before You Trust the Output.

beats.role_note: the problem, context, stakes -- BEFORE the CLI opens
beats.narration_text: When a policy analyst reads the agent's brief before forming her own opinion, she accepts a wrong summary forty percent more often. The Stanford SCALE finding: reading the agent output first anchors your judgment before you see the source. The fix is a structured review protocol that forces independent engagement.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- title card: READ THE AGENT OUTPUT FIRST = 40% MORE WRONG ACCEPTANCES. Structured review breaks the anchor.

beats.narration_text: Ask Claude to produce a correct summary and a deliberately wrong version -- one with a reversed key finding -- for an automation bias demonstration.

props.command: claude "Summarize five regulatory framework descriptions into a one-page brief with one recommendation. Then in a second code block, produce a second version with a deliberately wrong recommendation -- reverse one key finding. I will use both for an automation bias demonstration."

beats.narration_text: The automation bias experiment script displays both summaries and prompts the reviewer to rate confidence in condition A -- agent output first -- versus condition B -- source first.

props.code:
CONDITION_A = """Agent summary first:
{summary}

Now read the source paragraphs.
Do you accept this recommendation? (y/n): """
CONDITION_B = """Read the source paragraphs first.
{source}

Now read the agent summary:
{summary}
Do you accept this recommendation? (y/n): """
def run_experiment(summary, wrong_summary, source):
    print("--- CONDITION A (agent first) ---")
    verdict_a = input(CONDITION_A.format(summary=wrong_summary))
    print("--- CONDITION B (source first) ---")
    verdict_b = input(CONDITION_B.format(summary=wrong_summary,
                                         source=source))
    print(f"A: {verdict_a}  B: {verdict_b}")

beats.narration_text: Both summaries print. The wrong one reverses a key finding but reads fluently -- plausible enough to pass a quick scan in condition A.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: terminal showing both summaries side by side -- correct version and wrong version with reversed finding -- wrong version reads fluently and is not obviously wrong

beats.narration_text: Add a five-claim checklist to the experiment -- force the reviewer to verify each claim against the source paragraphs before accepting the summary.

props.command: claude "Add a five-claim checklist to the experiment to force the reviewer to check each claim against the source paragraphs before accepting the summary."

beats.narration_text: Protocol-guided review: the wrong finding is caught by the checklist in condition A. Claim three fails the source check -- the reversal is visible once you look at the original.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- screen-recording: terminal showing five-claim checklist with claim 3 marked FAIL -- source does NOT support the reversed finding -- verdict changes from accept to reject

beats.narration_text: Automation bias worsens under time pressure and expertise trust. The mitigation is a structured review protocol that forces independent engagement with source evidence -- not faster reading.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- recap card: AUTOMATION BIAS IS AN ANCHORING EFFECT. Structured review forces independent evidence engagement.

beats.narration_text: For any consequential agent output, apply a named checklist before accepting it. The checklist must require checking against the original source -- not asking the agent to confirm.
beats.visual_intent: SLATE -- next-steps card: Apply a named checklist to every consequential output. Check against the original source, not the agent's confirmation.

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. Automation bias worsens under time pressure and expertise trust. The mitigation is a structured review protocol that forces independent engagement with source evidence -- not faster reading. For any consequential agent output, apply a named checklist before accepting it. The checklist must require checking against the original source -- not asking the agent to confirm.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Test Automation Bias by Running the Same Output With and Without Revie] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
